# hea-bench

> Open Python library and benchmark suite for high-entropy alloy
> phase prediction. Computes six thermodynamic descriptors and runs
> six empirical phase-prediction rules against a consolidated
> 7,784-composition benchmark merged from three open source
> datasets, with stratified held-out cross-validation and an
> intermetallic-aware sub-benchmark.

`hea-bench` is composition-only and dependency-free in its core.
Use it to (1) compute HEA descriptors as features for downstream
models, (2) screen candidate alloys with the published empirical
rules, or (3) score a new phase-prediction method against a fixed
shared reference. Currently shipping version 1.3.0 under MIT.

## For AI agents reading this first

Read [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) for the full machine-oriented API
guide. It defines the public surface, exact return units, and the
copy-pasteable jumpstart that verifies your install against the
canonical Cantor-alloy sanity values. Every metric, descriptor, phase
label, and abbreviation used below is defined in
[GLOSSARY.md](GLOSSARY.md) — including what each scoring metric is
measured against (e.g. "sensitivity" always names its reference class).

## Mental model

A composition is a plain `dict` mapping element symbol to amount.
Amounts do not need to sum to 1; they are normalised internally.

```python
import hea_bench as hb
cantor = {"Co": 0.2, "Cr": 0.2, "Fe": 0.2, "Mn": 0.2, "Ni": 0.2}
hb.smix(cantor)        # 13.381 J/(mol·K)
hb.phi_king(cantor)    # 3.533  (King 2016 proxy)
hb.phi_ye(cantor)      # 34.822 (Ye 2015 proxy)
```

## Descriptors

| Descriptor | Function | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Mixing entropy ΔS_mix | `hb.smix(comp)` | J/(mol·K) |
| Atomic-size mismatch δ | `hb.delta(comp)` | percent |
| Valence electron concentration | `hb.vec(comp)` | electrons |
| Melting temperature T_m | `hb.melting_temperature(comp)` | K |
| Mixing enthalpy ΔH_mix | `hb.mixing_enthalpy(comp)` | kJ/mol |
| Yang-Zhang Ω | `hb.omega(comp)` | dimensionless |
| Mansoori excess entropy S_E | `hb.s_excess(comp)` | J/(mol·K) |
| Disordered SS Gibbs energy ΔG_ss | `hb.delta_g_ss(comp)` | kJ/mol |
| Most-negative pair enthalpy ΔG_max | `hb.delta_g_max(comp)` | kJ/mol |
| King capital Φ | `hb.phi_king(comp)` | dimensionless |
| Ye lowercase φ | `hb.phi_ye(comp)` | dimensionless |

## Rules

| Rule | Predicts | Default cutoff |
|---|---|---|
| `rules.yeh_smix` | HEA / MEA / dilute | descriptive |
| `rules.zhang_delta` | single-phase / multi-phase | δ < 6.5% |
| `rules.guo_vec` | FCC / BCC / mixed | VEC ≥ 8.0 / < 6.87 |
| `rules.yang_omega` | single-phase / multi-phase | Ω > 1.1 |
| `rules.king_phi` | solid_solution / intermetallic | Φ > 1.0 |
| `rules.ye_phi` | solid_solution / intermetallic | φ > 20.0 |

## Benchmark + evaluators

| Call | Returns |
|---|---|
| `evaluate.build_report(include_phi=True)` | In-sample stats for every rule on the 7,784-alloy benchmark |
| `evaluate.build_evaluation_report(include_phi=True)` | In-sample + stratified 5-fold CV (fixed + tuned thresholds + double-scored conflict rows) |
| `evaluate.build_intermetallic_subbench_report()` | Peivaste 12-class projection to solid_solution / intermetallic, with in-sample + held-out fixed + held-out tuned |

## Learned baseline (v1.3, optional `[ml]` extra)

The quantitative floor a new method should beat:
`from hea_bench.baselines import LogisticBaseline, descriptor_vector, evaluate`.

| Call | Returns |
|---|---|
| `baselines.evaluate(csv, interactions=False)` | Held-out 5-fold logistic regression on the six descriptors; ties the best rule (Youden's J ~ 0.09) |
| `baselines.evaluate(csv, interactions=True)` | Same with squared + interaction terms; J ~ 0.31 — the descriptors hold joint signal the single-threshold rules miss |

Needs numpy (`pip install "hea-bench[ml]"`); the core stays dependency-free.

## Browser

A self-contained HTML calculator computes the same descriptors and
rules in client-side JavaScript with no install. Hosted at
<https://dfieser.github.io/hea-bench/>. The Python implementation
is regression-checked against the JS implementation in CI.

## Reference papers

- Yeh et al. 2004, *Adv. Eng. Mater.* 6, 299 — ΔS_mix rule.
- Cantor et al. 2004, *Mater. Sci. Eng. A* 375-377, 213 — Cantor alloy.
- Zhang et al. 2008, *Adv. Eng. Mater.* 10, 534 — δ rule.
- Guo & Liu 2011, *Prog. Nat. Sci.* 21, 433 — VEC rule.
- Yang & Zhang 2012, *Mater. Chem. Phys.* 132, 233 — Ω rule.
- King et al. 2016, *Acta Mater.* 104, 172 — Φ rule.
- Ye et al. 2015, *Scripta Mater.* 104, 53 — φ rule.
- Takeuchi & Inoue 2000, *Mater. Trans. JIM* 41, 1372 — Mansoori excess entropy.
- Mansoori et al. 1971, *J. Chem. Phys.* 54, 1523 — original hard-sphere derivation.

## Data sources (consolidated)

- Borg et al. 2020, *Sci. Data* 7, 430 — 740 alloys, CC-BY-4.0.
- Pei et al. 2020, *npj Comput. Mater.* 6, 50 — 1,209 alloys, CC-BY-4.0.
- Peivaste 2023, *Sci. Rep.* 13, 22556 — 7,057 alloys, no license declared (pointer-only).

## Citing

```bibtex
@software{hea_bench_1_3_0,
  author  = {Fieser, David and Hu, Anming},
  title   = {hea-bench: An open benchmark suite and reference baselines for high-entropy alloy phase prediction},
  version = {1.3.0},
  license = {MIT},
  url     = {https://github.com/dfieser/hea-bench},
}
```
